content_markdown: A point source of light of amplitude $A_0$ gives rise to a particular light intensity when viewed at a distance from the source. When the amplitude is increased and the viewing distance is doubled, the light intensity is doubled. What is the new amplitude of the source?

diagram_metadata.description: No diagram provided in the image.

vibe_explanation: Intensity $I$ is proportional to the square of the amplitude $A$ at the source ($I \propto A^2$) and inversely proportional to the square of the distance $r$ ($I \propto 1/r^2$). Combining these, $I \propto A^2 / r^2$. Initially, $I_1 = k A_0^2 / r^2$. In the second case, the distance is $2r$ and the intensity is $2I_1$. So, $2I_1 = k A_{new}^2 / (2r)^2$. Substituting $I_1$, we get $2(k A_0^2 / r^2) = k A_{new}^2 / 4r^2$. Simplifying gives $2 A_0^2 = A_{new}^2 / 4$, which means $A_{new}^2 = 8 A_0^2$. Taking the square root, $A_{new} = \sqrt{8} A_0 = 2\sqrt{2} A_0$.
